title: Why we always sit in the same seat at meetings
summary: Explore the habit of claiming the same chair at regular meetings or shared spaces, and consider what this small territorial behaviour reveals about human psychology.
topic: Why we always sit in the same seat at meetings
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) exploring the habit of sitting in the same seat whenever you attend a regular meeting or visit a familiar space. Describe the habit, consider why people do it, and reflect on what it reveals about routine and comfort. Use present perfect, modal verbs, and at least one sentence beginning with 'Although' or 'Even though'.
hintStarters: Even though the seats are identical and no one has reserved them…
The first time I noticed I had developed this habit was when…
What surprises me is not that people do this, but how strongly they feel…

hintVocab.meaningVi: tính có thể dự đoán được
một thói quen ít rủi ro

sampleText:
There is a weekly team meeting at a publishing house in Seoul where twelve people gather around a rectangular table every Monday morning. Nobody has assigned seats, the chairs are identical, and the agenda changes every week. Yet after the first few sessions, each person had quietly settled into a position they now treat as their own. The editor who sits nearest the window has sat there for three years. When a new colleague accidentally took her chair on his first day, she spent the entire meeting looking mildly uncomfortable, though she said nothing.

Although this behaviour might seem trivial, it reflects something real about how people manage uncertainty. Choosing a seat is a small decision, but in a meeting room it combines with several other small decisions: where to put your bag, who you will end up facing, how visible you will be to whoever is presenting. Settling all of these variables the same way each time reduces the cognitive load of the meeting before it has even started. The seat becomes a familiar anchor. You know what the light will be like, which colleague tends to talk too much from the corner, and how far you are from the whiteboard. Having this information without needing to process it again frees a small but genuine amount of attention for the actual content of the meeting.

What is interesting is the mild distress people feel when someone takes their usual spot. It is clearly not a serious problem — the seat has no greater claim on them than on anyone else — yet they have often described the sensation as being slightly displaced, as though the room has been rearranged without warning. That reaction suggests that the habit has done its work: the seat has become, subconsciously, a small piece of claimed territory. The unspoken rule in most offices is that no one mentions this openly, which may be why it persists so reliably. We have built a parallel seating plan that exists only in habit, enforced by no one and yet almost universally respected.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: had quietly settled into a position
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đã lặng lẽ ổn định vào một vị trí
cách mọi người quản lý sự không chắc chắn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: frees a small but genuine amount of attention
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: giải phóng một lượng chú ý nhỏ nhưng thực sự

sampleAnnotations.phrase: has no greater claim on them
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: không có quyền sở hữu lớn hơn
lãnh thổ đã được chiếm lấy
tồn tại một cách đáng tin cậy

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to show a habit that had already formed
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'after the first few sessions, each person had quietly settled into a position' — quá khứ hoàn thành ('had settled') cho thấy hành động hoàn tất trước một thời điểm khác trong quá khứ ('after the first few sessions'). Nó cho thấy thói quen đã hình thành trước khi câu chuyện được kể.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'after the first few sessions, each person had quietly settled into a position' — past perfect ('had settled') shows the action completed before another past point ('after the first few sessions'). It shows the habit had already formed before the narrative moment described.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal 'might' for tentative observation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although this behaviour might seem trivial, it reflects something real' — 'might seem' dùng modal 'might' để thừa nhận rằng người đọc có thể không đồng ý, trước khi đưa ra lập luận phản bác. Đây là kỹ thuật hữu ích trong văn viết học thuật và cá nhân ở cấp độ B1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although this behaviour might seem trivial, it reflects something real' — 'might seem' uses the modal 'might' to acknowledge that the reader may disagree before the writer presents a counter-argument. This is a useful B1 technique for balanced, thoughtful writing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một cuộc họp nhóm hàng tuần tại một nhà xuất bản ở Seoul nơi mười hai người tụ tập quanh một chiếc bàn hình chữ nhật mỗi sáng thứ Hai. Không ai được phân chỗ ngồi, các ghế đều giống hệt nhau, và chương trình nghị sự thay đổi mỗi tuần. Vậy mà sau vài buổi đầu tiên, mỗi người đã lặng lẽ ổn định vào một vị trí mà họ giờ đây coi như của mình. Biên tập viên ngồi gần cửa sổ nhất đã ngồi ở đó ba năm. Khi một đồng nghiệp mới vô tình ngồi vào ghế của cô ấy vào ngày đầu tiên, cô ấy đã ngồi suốt buổi họp với vẻ hơi khó chịu, dù không nói gì.

Mặc dù hành vi này có vẻ tầm thường, nhưng nó phản ánh điều gì đó thực sự về cách mọi người quản lý sự không chắc chắn. Chọn chỗ ngồi là một quyết định nhỏ, nhưng trong phòng họp nó kết hợp với vài quyết định nhỏ khác: để túi ở đâu, cuối cùng bạn sẽ đối mặt với ai, bạn có dễ nhìn thấy từ người đang thuyết trình không. Giải quyết tất cả các biến số này theo cách giống nhau mỗi lần giảm tải nhận thức của cuộc họp trước khi nó bắt đầu. Chiếc ghế trở thành một điểm neo quen thuộc. Bạn biết ánh sáng sẽ như thế nào, đồng nghiệp nào thường nói quá nhiều từ góc kia, và bạn cách xa bảng trắng bao xa. Có thông tin này mà không cần xử lý lại giải phóng một lượng chú ý nhỏ nhưng thực sự cho nội dung thực sự của cuộc họp.

Điều thú vị là sự khó chịu nhỏ mà mọi người cảm thấy khi ai đó ngồi vào chỗ quen của họ. Rõ ràng đó không phải là vấn đề nghiêm trọng — chiếc ghế không có quyền sở hữu lớn hơn đối với họ so với bất kỳ ai khác — nhưng họ thường mô tả cảm giác như bị chiếm chỗ một chút, như thể căn phòng đã được sắp xếp lại mà không có cảnh báo. Phản ứng đó cho thấy thói quen đã hoàn thành công việc của nó: chiếc ghế đã trở thành, một cách vô thức, một mảnh lãnh thổ nhỏ đã được chiếm lấy. Quy tắc bất thành văn ở hầu hết các văn phòng là không ai đề cập đến điều này một cách công khai, đó có thể là lý do tại sao nó tồn tại một cách đáng tin cậy như vậy. Chúng ta đã xây dựng một sơ đồ chỗ ngồi song song chỉ tồn tại trong thói quen, không ai thực thi nhưng hầu như được tôn trọng phổ quát.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'might' to make a tentative observation?
options: Although this behaviour might seem trivial, it reflects something real.
Although this behaviour might seems trivial, it reflects something real.
Although this behaviour might to seem trivial, it reflects something real.
Although this behaviour might seemed trivial, it reflects something real.
mcQuestions.explanation: After a modal verb ('might'), use the bare infinitive 'seem', not 'seems', 'to seem', or 'seemed'.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal verb + bare infinitive: might + seem (no -s, no to, no -ed).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the past perfect to show a completed background habit?
options: After the first few sessions, each person had quietly settled into a position.
After the first few sessions, each person has quietly settled into a position.
After the first few sessions, each person quietly had settled into a position.
After the first few sessions, each person settled into a position had.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Had settled' (past perfect) shows the habit was already formed before the narrative's main past timeline.
mcQuestions.hint: For a completed action before another past moment: had + past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'treat + object + as' in the present?
options: They treat their seat as their own.
They treat their seat like their own.
They treat their seat as if it is their own.
They treat their seat as their own's.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Treat + object + as + noun phrase' is the standard construction. 'Like' would be colloquial; 'as if' changes meaning; 'their own's' is not a word.
mcQuestions.hint: Standard: treat something as something — not 'like', not 'as if'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a present perfect to describe a long-standing situation?
options: The editor who sits nearest the window has sat there for three years.
The editor who sits nearest the window has been sitting there since three years.
The editor who sits nearest the window sat there for three years.
The editor who sits nearest the window is sitting there for three years.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has sat' + 'for three years' uses present perfect to describe a situation that started in the past and still continues.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect + 'for' + duration: has sat for three years.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'though' correctly as a sentence-final concessive?
options: She spent the entire meeting looking mildly uncomfortable, though she said nothing.
She spent the entire meeting looking mildly uncomfortable, although she said nothing.
She spent the entire meeting looking mildly uncomfortable. Though, she said nothing.
She spent the entire meeting looking mildly uncomfortable though said nothing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Though' can be used at the end of a clause to add a concession informally; 'although' works here too, but 'though' sentence-finally is more natural.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Though' at the end of a clause is a natural, informal concessive marker.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'tend to' in the present simple?
options: One colleague tends to talk too much from the corner.
One colleague tend to talk too much from the corner.
One colleague tends talking too much from the corner.
One colleague tends to talking too much from the corner.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to + bare infinitive' with third-person -s: 'tends to talk'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Tend to' + bare infinitive; third-person singular adds -s to 'tend'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly describes a past recurring pattern with 'have + past participle'?
options: They have often described the sensation as being slightly displaced.
They have often describe the sensation as being slightly displaced.
They often have described the sensation as being slightly displaced.
They often described the sensation as being slightly displaced.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have + past participle' with 'often' describes a repeated past action with present relevance; 'often' sits between the auxiliary and past participle.
mcQuestions.hint: Frequency adverb placement: have + often + past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'before + gerund' in a time clause?
options: The cognitive load is reduced before the meeting has even started.
The cognitive load is reduced before the meeting is even starting.
The cognitive load is reduced before the meeting had even started.
The cognitive load is reduced before the meeting even starts.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple is used in time clauses after 'before' when the main clause is also present: 'is reduced before it starts'.
mcQuestions.hint: Time clauses after 'before': use present simple (not future or perfect).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'no one' + verb agreement?
options: No one mentions this openly.
No one mention this openly.
No ones mention this openly.
No one are mentioning this openly.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'No one' is singular; the verb takes the third-person singular form 'mentions'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a passive construction?
options: It is enforced by no one and yet almost universally respected.
It is enforced by no one and yet almost universally respecting.
It is enforced by no one and yet almost universal respected.
It enforces by no one and yet almost universally respected.
mcQuestions.explanation: Passive: 'is enforced' and 'respected' — both are past participles; 'universally' is the adverb modifying 'respected'.
mcQuestions.hint: Passive: is/are + past participle; use adverb 'universally' before 'respected'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'suggest + that clause' in formal writing?
options: That reaction suggests that the habit has done its work.
That reaction suggests the habit has did its work.
That reaction suggests that the habit have done its work.
That reaction is suggesting that the habit has done its work.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Suggest + that + subject + present perfect' is the standard structure; 'has done' agrees with singular 'habit'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Suggest that': use present perfect with correct subject-verb agreement.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Do you think this kind of seat-claiming behaviour is more about comfort, status, or control? Can you think of other situations where people create unofficial rules in shared spaces?
